title: The Need for a Better Integral

content.text: The Riemann integral has served us well, but it has fundamental limitations:

content.title: The Dirichlet Function Is Not Riemann Integrable
content.problem:
Recall the Dirichlet function:
\[
D(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x \in \mathbb{Q} \\ 0 & x \notin \mathbb{Q} \end{cases}
\]
For any partition $P$, every subinterval contains both rationals and 
irrationals. Thus $M_i = 1$ and $m_i = 0$ for all $i$, giving 
$U(D, P) = 1$ and $L(D, P) = 0$ for every $P$.

content.text:
Yet "intuitively," the integral should be $0$: the rationals are a 
"negligible" subset of $[0, 1]$.

content.title: Pointwise Limits May Not Be Integrable
content.problem:
Let $\{r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots\}$ enumerate the rationals in $[0, 1]$. Define
\[
f_n(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x \in \{r_1, \ldots, r_n\} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\]
Each $f_n$ is Riemann integrable (with $\int f_n = 0$). But $f_n \to D$ 
pointwise, and $D$ is not Riemann integrable.

content.text: What Properties Should a Good Measure Have?

We seek a function $m: \mathcal{S} \to [0, \infty]$ assigning a "measure" 
to sets in some collection $\mathcal{S}$, satisfying:

items:
**Countable additivity:** If $A_1, A_2, \ldots$ are disjoint 
sets in $\mathcal{S}$, then $m\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n\right) = \sum_{n=1}^\infty m(A_n)$.

content.text:
It turns out that no such measure can be defined on *all* subsets of 
$\mathbb{R}$ (assuming the Axiom of Choice). But it can be defined on a 
very large collection called the *measurable sets*.

The strategy: define an "outer measure" on all sets (erring on the side 
of overestimation), then restrict to sets where the outer measure behaves 
additively.

content.title: Outer Measure (Preview)
content.content:
For any set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$:
\[
m^*(A) = \inf\left\{\sum_{n=1}^\infty |I_n| : A \subseteq \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty I_n, \text{ each } I_n \text{ an interval}\right\}
\]
where $|I_n|$ denotes the length of interval $I_n$.

content.text:
This measures $A$ by covering it with intervals and taking the infimum 
of total length over all covers.
